example","user":{"login":"udohjeremiah","id":98605632,"node_id":"U_kgDOBeCaQA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98605632?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah","html_url":"https://github.com/udohjeremiah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"IMO, the current example on the [Optional Arguments](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/functions/#Optional-Arguments) section of the [function section of the documentation](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/functions/) is too complex and it introduces a lot of ambiguity for people following up with the docs from the beginning to the function section.

My reasons are as follows:
1. It introduces too much of another section of the docs into a section, when nothing was said yet about the former section. The section on **"Modules"** is introduced into the **"Functions"** section, when nothing has been said yet about modules. This then goes on to introduce a few noteworthy things:
    - for users reading the documentation stage by stage from the beginning or complete beginners to programming, they can't understand or follow up with the example, to see what's happening inside the function.

2. The example itself isn't complete and can't be copied and pasted into the REPL to see what's happening, causing more confusion for beginners:
```julia
julia> function Date(y::Int64, m::Int64=1, d::Int64=1)
           err = validargs(Date, y, m, d)
           err === nothing || throw(err)
           return Date(UTD(totaldays(y, m, d)))
       end
Date (generic function with 3 methods)

julia> using Dates
WARNING: using Dates.Date in module Main conflicts with an existing identifier.

julia> Date(2000, 12, 12)
ERROR: UndefVarError: validargs not defined

julia> Date(2000, 12)
ERROR: UndefVarError: validargs not defined

julia> Date(2000)
ERROR: UndefVarError: validargs not defined
```

3. The statement made here in that section: **"Observe, that this definition calls another method of the Date function that takes one argument of type UTInstant{Day}."**. For users not exposed to the `Date` module yet, this isn't understand and people can't follow up with it.

4. Then there is a closing statement there: **"This can be checked for our Date function example by calling methods function."** An example wasn't provided for this, which is understand as the `Date` isn't a **"real"** function. Using a custom method will enable showing this right away and then users can see what's being said.

My point is this: Since this is talking about **"Optional Arguments"** and its introducing functions to beginners of programming, giving a simple function with optional arguments would enable beginners and people reading the docs stage by stage easily follow with the the current example on the [Optional Arguments](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/functions/#Optional-Arguments) section of the [function section of the documentation](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/functions/) is too complex and it introduces a lot of ambiguity for people following up with the docs from the beginning to the function section.

My reasons are as follows:
1. It introduces too much of another section of the docs into a section, when nothing was said yet about the former section. The section on **"Modules"** is introduced into the **"Functions"** section, when nothing has been said yet about modules. This then goes on to introduce a few noteworthy things:
    - for users reading the documentation stage by stage from the beginning or complete beginners to programming, they can't understand or follow up with the example, to see what's happening inside the function.

2. The example itself isn't complete and can't be copied and pasted into the REPL to see what's happening, causing more confusion for beginners:
```julia
julia> function Date(y::Int64, m::Int64=1, d::Int64=1)
           err = validargs(Date, y, m, d)
           err === nothing || throw(err)
           return Date(UTD(totaldays(y, m, d)))
       end
Date (generic function with 3 methods)

julia> using Dates
WARNING: using Dates.Date in module Main conflicts with an existing identifier.

julia> Date(2000, 12, 12)
ERROR: UndefVarError: validargs not defined

julia> Date(2000, 12)
ERROR: UndefVarError: validargs not defined

julia> Date(2000)
ERROR: UndefVarError: validargs not defined
```

3. The statement made here in that section: **"Observe, that this definition calls another method of the Date function that takes one argument of type UTInstant{Day}."**. For users not exposed to the `Date` module yet, this isn't understand and people can't follow up with it.

4. Then there is a closing statement there: **"This can be checked for our Date function example by calling methods function."** An example wasn't provided for this, which is understand as the `Date` isn't a **"real"** function. Using a custom method will enable showing this right away and then users can see what's being said.

My point is this: Since this is talking about **"Optional Arguments"** and its introducing functions to beginners of programming, giving a simple function with optional arguments would enable beginners and people reading the docs stage by stage easily follow with the example","user":{"login":"udohjeremiah","id":98605632,"node_id":"U_kgDOBeCaQA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98605632?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah","html_url":"https://github.com/udohjeremiah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This part of the doc under [Argument-type declarations](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/functions/#Argument-type-declarations) states this:

> You can declare the types of function arguments by appending ::TypeName to the argument name, as usual for [Type Declarations](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/types/#Type-Declarations) in Julia. For example, the following function computes [Fibonacci numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) recursively:
>
>fib(n::Integer) = n ≤ 2 ? one(n) : fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
>
>and the ::Integer specification means that it will only be callable when n is a subtype of the [abstract](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/types/#man-abstract-types) Integer type.

As the title states it's main aim is to discuss **"Argument-Type Declarations"**, not **"Recursive Functions"**. I explicitly stated **"Recursive Functions"** because that function seems to distract any "naive user" looking at that part. By "naive users" I mean first time programmers whose first language is Julia.

Assuming they're looking at functions for the first time, this would complicate things for people. There is a simple `while` loop to execute Fibonacci numbers, which is clear and "naive users" can follow through easily to see what's happening (since they won't only be looking at just the `::` there). Why bring up the complexity of recursive functions into a section trying to explain something to a newbie.

To add, I would love if the community not only aims at making Julia "the fastest language", but it should also strive for "the simplest language to learn". Please this is not comparison, I'm just trying to point what I'm trying to say: for example, people often say "Python is so simple to learn and use", and you can see how far they go to make sure things are dead simple. An example [here](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/introduction.html#first-steps-towards-programming) is how they show Fibonacci numbers on the docs:
```python
>>> # Fibonacci series:
... # the sum of two elements defines the next
... a, b = 0, 1
>>> while a < 10:
...     print(a)
...     a, b = b, a+b
...
0
1
1
2
3
5
8
```

While I know a lot of Julia users are "high-thinkers" coming from LISP, we should try and make things simple when possible, considering that people are now learning programming with Julia as their first Furthermore, the repeated use of appeal to emotion in order to justify the proposed change is not a helpful path.

There is a logical reason why the `while` loop version should be avoided: up to this point in a linear reading of the manual, control flow constructs are yet to be introduced. This is very likely intentional. Consider a complete novice -- what basis would there be to believe that they would understand the termination criterion of a while loop? (e.g. is the condition evaluated at the entrance or exit of the loop?) A problem as simple as the scoping of `n` in the loop is a valid point of confusion for a complete novice. 
Recursion avoids these subtle questions, as the entry/exit points of the function provide clear demarcations.

My position remains that the original version serves purpose quite well and does not need replacement. I fail to understand how recursion is such a big deal, even for a complete example","user":{"login":"udohjeremiah","id":98605632,"node_id":"U_kgDOBeCaQA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98605632?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah","html_url":"https://github.com/udohjeremiah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This part of the doc under [Argument-type declarations](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/functions/#Argument-type-declarations) states this:

> You can declare the types of function arguments by appending ::TypeName to the argument name, as usual for [Type Declarations](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/types/#Type-Declarations) in Julia. For example, the following function computes [Fibonacci numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) recursively:
>
>fib(n::Integer) = n ≤ 2 ? one(n) : fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
>
>and the ::Integer specification means that it will only be callable when n is a subtype of the [abstract](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/types/#man-abstract-types) Integer type.

As the title states it's main aim is to discuss **"Argument-Type Declarations"**, not **"Recursive Functions"**. I explicitly stated **"Recursive Functions"** because that function seems to distract any "naive user" looking at that part. By "naive users" I mean first time programmers whose first language is Julia.

Assuming they're looking at functions for the first time, this would complicate things for people. There is a simple `while` loop to execute Fibonacci numbers, which is clear and "naive users" can follow through easily to see what's happening (since they won't only be looking at just the `::` there). Why bring up the complexity of recursive functions into a section trying to explain something to a newbie.

To add, I would love if the community not only aims at making Julia "the fastest language", but it should also strive for "the simplest language to learn". Please this is not comparison, I'm just trying to point what I'm trying to say: for example, people often say "Python is so simple to learn and use", and you can see how far they go to make sure things are dead simple. An example [here](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/introduction.html#first-steps-towards-programming) is how they show Fibonacci numbers on the docs:
```python
>>> # Fibonacci series:
... # the sum of two elements defines the next
... a, b = 0, 1
>>> while a < 10:
...     print(a)
...     a, b = b, a+b
...
0
1
1
2
3
5
8
```

While I know a lot of Julia users are "high-thinkers" coming from LISP, we should try and make things simple when possible, considering that people are now learning programming with Julia as their first ,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Remove example","user":{"login":"udohjeremiah","id":98605632,"node_id":"U_kgDOBeCaQA","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98605632?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah","html_url":"https://github.com/udohjeremiah","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/udohjeremiah/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This part of the doc under [Argument-type declarations](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/functions/#Argument-type-declarations) states this:

> You can declare the types of function arguments by appending ::TypeName to the argument name, as usual for [Type Declarations](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/types/#Type-Declarations) in Julia. For example, the following function computes [Fibonacci numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) recursively:
>
>fib(n::Integer) = n ≤ 2 ? one(n) : fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
>
>and the ::Integer specification means that it will only be callable when n is a subtype of the [abstract](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/types/#man-abstract-types) Integer type.

As the title states it's main aim is to discuss **"Argument-Type Declarations"**, not **"Recursive Functions"**. I explicitly stated **"Recursive Functions"** because that function seems to distract any "naive user" looking at that part. By "naive users" I mean first time programmers whose first language is Julia.

Assuming they're looking at functions for the first time, this would complicate things for people. There is a simple `while` loop to execute Fibonacci numbers, which is clear and "naive users" can follow through easily to see what's happening (since they won't only be looking at just the `::` there). Why bring up the complexity of recursive functions into a section trying to explain something to a newbie.

To add, I would love if the community not only aims at making Julia "the fastest language", but it should also strive for "the simplest language to learn". Please this is not comparison, I'm just trying to point what I'm trying to say: for example, people often say "Python is so simple to learn and use", and you can see how far they go to make sure things are dead simple. An example [here](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/introduction.html#first-steps-towards-programming) is how they show Fibonacci numbers on the docs:
```python
>>> # Fibonacci series:
... # the sum of two elements defines the next
... a, b = 0, 1
>>> while a < 10:
...     print(a)
...     a, b = b, a+b
...
0
1
1
2
3
5
8
```

While I know a lot of Julia users are "high-thinkers" coming from LISP, we should try and make things simple when possible, considering that people are now learning programming with Julia as their first https://discourse.julialang.org/t/weird-behaviour-of-setdiff/88110","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47006/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47006/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1276880332","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47006#issuecomment-1276880332","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47006","id":1276880332,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5MG6XM","user":{"login":"andrewjradcliffe","id":96091198,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbo8Pg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96091198?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe","html_url":"https://github.com/andrewjradcliffe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-10-13T00:33:58Z","updated_at":"2022-10-13T00:33:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"To enumerate the set (no pun intended) of all valid inputs and outputs would be quite strange in a language such as Julia which explicitly goes to great lengths to enable generic programming. Furthermore, it would imply limitations which do not exist. 

In fact, all that you really need for the set operators to work on arbitrarily complex data types is to define `hash` for your custom type (of course, there are often more efficient approaches).

An example
```julia
julia> struct MyType{T, U}
           x::T
           y::U
       end

julia> Base.hash(t::MyType, h::UInt) = hash(t.x, hash(t.y, h))

julia> v1 = [MyType(1, 1.5), MyType(2, 3.0), MyType(2, 1.5)]
3-element Vector{MyType{Int64, Float64}}:
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(1, 1.5)
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 3.0)
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 1.5)

julia> v2 = [MyType(2, 1.5), MyType(2, 3.0)]
2-element Vector{MyType{Int64, Float64}}:
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 1.5)
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 3.0)

julia> setdiff(v1, v2)
1-element Vector{MyType{Int64, Float64}}:
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(1, 1.5)

julia> t1 = Tuple(v1); t2 = Tuple(v2);

julia> setdiff(t1, t2)
1-element Vector{MyType{Int64, Float64}}:
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(1, 1.5)

julia> t1 ∩ v2
2-element Vector{MyType{Int64, Float64}}:
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 3.0)
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 1.5)

julia> symdiff(v1, t2)
1-element Vector{MyType{Int64, Float64}}:
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(1, 1.5)

julia> t1 ∪ t2 ⊇ t2 ⊇ t1 ∩ v2
true

julia> d = Dict(zip((:a, :b, :c), v1))
Dict{Symbol, MyType{Int64, Float64}} with 3 entries:
  :a => MyType{Int64, Float64}(1, 1.5)
  :b => MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 3.0)
  :c => MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 1.5)

julia> setdiff(values(d), t2)
1-element Vector{MyType{Int64, Float64}}:
 MyType{Int64, Float64}(1, 1.5)

julia> nt = (; zip([:a, :b], v2)...)
(a = MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 1.5), b = MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 3.0))

julia> d ∩ pairs(nt)
1-element Vector{Pair{Symbol, MyType{Int64, Float64}}}:
 :b => MyType{Int64, Float64}(2, 3.0)

```

Maybe just a case of adding it to these cases? https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/35191ad5bd90a6068a90016cfda542fe467ecf28/base/reduce.jl#L346","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47231/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47231/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1284841549","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47231#issuecomment-1284841549","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47231","id":1284841549,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5MlSBN","user":{"login":"andrewjradcliffe","id":96091198,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbo8Pg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96091198?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe","html_url":"https://github.com/andrewjradcliffe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-10-20T02:52:58Z","updated_at":"2022-10-20T02:52:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I can give a partial explanation. Only a few binary operators have a sensible neural element. Recall also that a binary operator need not be associative; `-`, `/`,`^` are some examples.

Now, we can simplify some cases, e.g. when reducing over an entire array (treating it as a vector), as we can make a reasonable claim as to what the first element should be, thereby alleviating the need for a neutral element. This is why expressions like `reduce(op, A)` will typically work -- the first element is assumed to be `first(A)`

However, in the multidimensional case, it is ambiguous as to what the first element should be given that our operator may not be associative -- if I say, for example, for `A ∈ ℝᴺˣᴹˣᴾ`, `reduce(op, A, dims=(1,2))`, do I intend that a first element be found on the first row of the first column of each slice? Perhaps. Given a binary operator without the associative property, however, this implies that I am okay with starting the computation with whatever value happens to the first element -- a seemingly random choice in the general case (it may make sense in a specific case where the values held in the array are intentionally ordered).
In essence, unless a binary operator is associative, there is no way to sensibly begin a computation, hence, the need for a user to supply an initial value.

See also the `reduce` docstring.
As for the neutrality of 0 w.r.t. `-`:
```julia
# with a neutral element
julia> a = [1, 2, 3];

julia> v0 = 0
0

julia> v1 = -(v0, a[1])
-1

julia> v2 = -(v1, a[2])
-3

julia> v3 = -(v2, a[3])
-6

# without a neutral element
julia> v1 = a[1]
1

julia> v2 = -(v1, a[2])
-1

julia> v3 = -(v2, a[3])
-4
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1284841549/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236","id":1415615638,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5UYJSW","number":47236,"title":"Function for restricting array type","user":{"login":"pddshk","id":43893794,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQzODkzNzk0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/43893794?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk","html_url":"https://github.com/pddshk","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/pddshk/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-10-19T21:39:44Z","updated_at":"2022-10-20T03:18:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Assuming I have a vector of type `Vector{Any}` but it contains values of concrete type only. This is common practice when obtaining matrices from files and databases. So there should be a function that would try to promote types of all elements and then converts `Vector{Any}` to `Vector{T}` where `T` is calculated type.

Possible solution:

```julia
function restricttype(v::Vector{T1}) where T1
    T2 = mapreduce(typeof, promote_type, v)
    T1 == T2 ? v : convert.(T2, v)
end
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1284859625","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236#issuecomment-1284859625","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/47236","id":1284859625,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5MlWbp","user":{"login":"andrewjradcliffe","id":96091198,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbo8Pg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96091198?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe","html_url":"https://github.com/andrewjradcliffe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-10-20T03:18:59Z","updated_at":"2022-10-20T03:18:59Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The general solution to instability arising from text files is to either inform the parser as to the type (if it can even be type stable), and/or use a function barrier which type-asserts about the eltype (and perhaps also the dimensionality).

From databases, most of the Julia interfaces should be able to confer type stability; you may need a function barrier if the database has bad set up (e.g. induces type instability via SQL NULL, or, just mixes data types for whatever nonsensical reason).

In both cases, just function barrier that mess and call it a day. Nothing that can be added to `Base` (or any package) can possibly anticipate all the weird, curious and plainly wrong ways that humans will mix data in text files, databases, etc...

A contrived example (we know the type that we intend to read in, so we could have passed `Int64` instead of `Any` to `readdlm`):
```julia
using DelimitedFiles
x = collect(reshape(1:15, 5, 3))
open("test.csv", "w") do io
    writedlm(io, x, ',')
end

function mybarrier(::Type{T}, io::AbstractString)::Matrix{T} where {T}
    # A = f(io) # do your here, e.g. below
    A = readdlm(io, ',', Any, '\n', header=false)
    convert(Matrix{T}, A)
end

@code_warntype mybarrier(Int64, "test.csv")
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1284859625/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

From [this](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/improving-performance-of-item-response-model-in-turing-jl/86249/21) Discourse thread, there is clear motivation for use of the Stan defaults; this directly affects the benchmark comparisons (see the examples in #484). 

I might suggest that if the Stan defaults are not used, an explanation be given. In the [v1.4](https://benchmarks.sciml.ai/html/ParameterEstimation/DiffEqBayesFitzHughNagumo.html) benchmarks, `stepsize_jitter=1` is a poignant example where misleading conclusions may be drawn. It is important to explain such choices, particularly when said choices are discouraged by the [Stan documentation](https://mc-stan.org/docs/cmdstan-guide/mcmc-config.html#algorithm).

PR fixs the possible error from `findmin/max` by ensuring that the input's `keys` contains no zero. (Close #38660)
The added check might be expensive (`zi in keys` calls `iterate` based fallback), but I can't find a non-breaking way to do the same check.

This PR also add a faster fallback if the input's `keys` is a `AbstractArray` with the same `axes`.
My local bench shows that it makes `findmin/max` almost 2x faster if `f` is cheap.

PR fixs the possible error from `findmin/max` by ensuring that the input's `keys` contains no zero. (Close #38660)
The added check might be expensive (`zi in keys` calls `iterate` based fallback), but I can't find a non-breaking way to do the same check.

This PR also add a faster fallback if the input's `keys` is a `AbstractArray` with the same `axes`.
My local bench shows that it makes `findmin/max` almost 2x faster if `f` is cheap.

for comment.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46060/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46060/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1186633924","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46060#issuecomment-1186633924","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46060","id":1186633924,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5GupjE","user":{"login":"andrewjradcliffe","id":96091198,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbo8Pg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96091198?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe","html_url":"https://github.com/andrewjradcliffe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-07-17T23:55:45Z","updated_at":"2022-07-17T23:55:45Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The following may help to explain.

Given an integer comprised of n bits, there are 2^n possible states if unsigned, 2^(n-1) possible states if signed and we restrict ourselves to non-negative numbers. If we wanted to generate a uniformly distributed rational on the unit interval with n-bit integers, then we need n uniformly distributed bits, if considering unsigned integers, or n-1 uniformly distributed bits, if considering signed integers. The probability mass associated with any numerator integer is 2^-n or 2^-(n-1), respectively. To produce a rational which is uniform on [0,1], normalize the numerator by the difference between the upper and lower bounds of the possible states, that is,
num / ((2^n - 1) - 0)    if unsigned
num / ((2^(n-1) - 1) - 0)    if signed

If  u ~ U(a,b) is a uniformly distributed random variable, the probability mass function is 1 / (b - a). Applying the transformation v = f(u) = u / c, the probability mass function of the transformed random variable is c / (b - a) = 1 / ((b/c) - (a/c)), that is, v ~ U(a/c, b/c). In other words, we recognize U(0, 2^n - 1) / ((2^n - 1) - 0) as U(0 / (2^n - 1), (2^n - 1) / (2^n - 1)). This demonstrates that the resultant random variable follows the desired distribution -- and therefore satisfies all the properties one might expect of it.

At first, this may look strange, if we attempt to think of the computer representation of a rational number as a real number. The values which may be represented using fixed-width integers are dictated by the size of said integers; likewise, the distance between rational numbers on an interval is fixed. Thus, it should not be surprising once we recognize the distribution of rationals on [0,1] for what it must invariably be: a discrete distribution. This becomes more poignant for small n, as the distance between rationals is large. On the other hand, for large n, the distance between rationals is small.

In fact, if one considers the anatomy of a floating point number, one finds the same discretization of the real line. Thus, any method (which relies on a stream of random bits) for generation of "real"s from the "continuous" uniform distribution on the unit interval is equivalent to the discrete distribution of rationals with n=p, where p is the precision. The fact that generation of floating point numbers on the unit interval has an open upper bound is an artifact of floating point representation, which do not admit a convenient means of including both 1.0 and 0.0 (when generating uniform numbers on unit interval). If there were a magic (read: equally efficient) way of generating uniform floats on the closed interval [0,1], it would likely be adopted. But, since the probability of actually drawing that 1.0 is 2^-p (or p-1, depending on type of RNG), it is viewed as an acceptable sacrifice.

Consider now an interesting argument: why not utilize the random generation of floats on [0,1) to generate random rationals? From the above, it should be clear that any floating point representation does not have an equivalent probability mass unless n=p (which is not true for any combination of fixed-width ints and floats, excluding float80); even if n=p, one must assume no rounding errors during the rationalization of the float.
This leads to a key insight, which is that rationalization of a floating point number, given n≠p, will produce values which are not uniformly distributed on the n-bit rationals, even if we assume no rounding errors. Error introduced by floating point rationalization will begin with rationals the numerator of which is > 2^p, as the distance between floats is greater than 1. In essence, taking Float64 (p=53) as an example, 2^53, 2^53 + 1, 2^53 + 2 are all valid numerators, but only the first and last can be represented as floating point numbers. This problem becomes increasingly severe as one moves toward large numbers, as the distance between floats grows exponentially. The effect is to place disproportionate probability mass on the rationals which admit exact floating point representations. And not a small portion of the unit interval of rationals is affected! -- for Float64, all the rationals with numerator > 2^53 are affected, that is, as a fraction of the interval, (2^64 - 2^53) / 2^64 if UInt64 or (2^63 - 2^53) / 2^63 if Int64. In general, the fraction of the unit interval affected will be, for p < n, (2^n - 2^p) / 2^n; substitute n=n-1 if using signed integers.
The resultant distribution of rationals will not be uniformly distributed, hence, rationalization of floating point random numbers on [0,1) as a means of generating uniform rationals is fundamentally flawed. In fact, explicit cautions should be given against such an approach. Another way to phrase this is that for any float greater than 2^p / 2^n (n=n-1 if signed), p < n, the probability mass will be incorrect.
(An interesting corollary is that uniform rationals with n=64 or n=63 are a better approximation to the continuous uniform distribution on the unit interval than Float64's).

Below, I provide a brief demonstration. I must remark that it was not my original intention to arrive at such a strong statement regarding the conversion of [0,1) floats to rationals, but the analysis bears what it must. As regards my original application, it is quite simple: I needed uniformly distributed rational numbers.

```julia
# Let's use Int64 since it is easier to read off than UInt64's
julia> m64 = Int64(maxintfloat(Float64)) # 2^53
9007199254740992

julia> r1 = m64 // typemax(Int64)
9007199254740992//9223372036854775807

julia> r2 = (m64 + 1) // typemax(Int64)
9007199254740993//9223372036854775807

julia> r3 = (m64 + 2) // typemax(Int64)
9007199254740994//9223372036854775807

julia> f1 = m64 / typemax(Int64)
0.0009765625

julia> f2 = (m64 + 1) / typemax(Int64)  # No hope since 2^53 + 1 is has no Float64 representation
0.0009765625

julia> f3 = (m64 + 2) / typemax(Int64)  # 2^53 + 2 does have a Float64 representation
0.0009765625000000002

julia> rationalize(Int64, f1, tol=eps()/2)
1//1024

julia> rationalize(Int64, f2, tol=eps()/2)
1//1024

julia> rationalize(Int64, f3, tol=eps()/2)
1//1024

julia> rationalize(Int64, f3, tol=eps()/10)
1//1024

julia> rationalize(Int64, f3, tol=eps()/100)
1//1024

julia> rationalize(Int64, f3, tol=eps()/1000)
1//1024

julia> rationalize(Int64, f3, tol=eps()/1025)    # We must use a tolerance < 2^-62
2200096473344//2252898788704255

# How much of the unit interval is affected?
julia> f(n, p) = (big(2)^n - big(2)^p) / big(2)^n;

julia> f(64, 53)    # 99.9% of the interval being wrong is pretty bad!
0.99951171875

julia> f(63, 53)
0.9990234375

# Or, another way to phrase it is: for any float > 2^p / 2^n, the probability mass will be incorrect.
julia> big(2)^53 / big(2)^64    # Threshold for UInt64
0.00048828125

julia> big(2)^53 / big(2)^63    # Threshold for Int64
0.0009765625

# Let's take a randomly generated Rational{Int64}
julia> num = 3929436088528050567
3929436088528050567

julia> den = typemax(Int)
9223372036854775807

julia> r = 3929436088528050567//9223372036854775807
3929436088528050567//9223372036854775807

julia> s = (3929436088528050567+1)//9223372036854775807
3929436088528050568//9223372036854775807

julia> float(r) == float(s)    # Obviously cannot distinguish between the float(r.num) and float(s.num)
true

julia> numf = Float64(num)
3.9294360885280507e18

julia> eps(numf)    # The nearest float (rounds up) -- far away!
512.0

julia> i_start = Int(prevfloat(numf))
3929436088528050176

julia> i_end = Int(numf)
3929436088528050688

julia> δ_back = num - i_start
391

julia> δ_forward = i_end - num
121

# If there were no other roundoff errors, then Float64(i_start + k) == Float64(i_end),
# for 256 < k < 768, but, alas, roundoff in the denominator as well causes this to
# not work out as neatly as one would like.
julia> s = (i_start + 257) // den      # k > 256 gets rounded up, hence, 256 rationals disappear
3929436088528050433//9223372036854775807

julia> float(s) == float(r)
true

julia> s = (i_start + 767) // den      # In total, 511 rationals are stacked into the same float
3929436088528050943//9223372036854775807

julia> float(s) == float(r)
true

# If we were to convert from the floating point representation, the rational corresponding
# to 0.42603031438250555 would exhibit a probability mass 510x greater than it should be.
julia> is = findall(float((i_start + k) // den) == float(r) for k = 257:767);

julia> ks = setdiff(257:767, (257:767)[is])
13-element Vector{Int64}:
 276
 349
 382
 422
 495
 568
 641
 714
 729
 743
 750
 757
 764

julia> s = (i_start + 276) // den
53827891623671924//126347562148695559

julia> float(s)    # rounds down, so not quite consistent, even in rounding.
0.4260303143825055

julia> float.((i_start .+ ks) .// den)    # sometimes rounds up before it should
13-element Vector{Float64}:
 0.4260303143825055
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825055
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825056
 0.42603031438250566
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825056
 0.4260303143825056

julia> float((i_start + 729) // den)      # more rounding than one would expect
0.42603031438250566

```

# On usage and flexibility
The above is rigid: n-bit rationals stored in n-bit integers, which, to perform subsequent arithmetic, necessitates a widening of the storage (e.g. `Rational{UInt64}` to `Rational{UInt128`) -- that is, an n-bit rational stored in an n-bit integer is on the precipice of overflow in most circumstances.
However, the above contains a kernel of a flexible idea: m-bit rationals stored in n-bit integers, where m ≤ n -- it reduces to the above at m=n. Such an approach enables one to select any discretization of the unit interval one desires.
A nice toy example can be n=8, m=3.
It does not require much modification to the methods -- I am not necessarily proposing that it be included as functionality in base, as it is a little awkward as phrased below:
```julia
function randrational(r::AbstractRNG, ::Type{Rational{T}}, m::Integer) where {T<:Union{UInt8,UInt16,UInt32,UInt64}}
    n = 8*sizeof(T)
    0 < m ≤ n || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof($T)"))
    x = rand(r, UInt64) >>> (64 - m) % T
    y = one(T) << m - one(T)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
function randrational(r::AbstractRNG, ::Type{Rational{T}}, m::Integer) where {T<:Union{Int8,Int16,Int32,Int64}}
    n = 8*sizeof(T)
    0 < m ≤ n-1 || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof($T)-1"))
    x = rand(r, UInt64) >>> (64 - m) % T
    y = typemax(T) >> (n-1 - m)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
function randrational(r::AbstractRNG, ::Type{Rational{UInt128}}, m::Integer)
    0 < m ≤ 128 || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof(UInt128)"))
    x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m)
    y = one(UInt128) << m - one(UInt128)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
function randrational(r::AbstractRNG, ::Type{Rational{Int128}}, m::Integer)
    0 < m ≤ 127 || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof(UInt128)-1"))
    x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m) % Int128
    y = typemax(Int128) >> (127 - m)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
randrational(::Type{Rational{T}}, m::Integer) where {T<:Integer} = randrational(default_rng(), Rational{T}, m)
```
An example (without hooking into `rand` interface)
```julia
julia> [randrational(Rational{UInt8}, 3) for _ = 1:10]
10-element Vector{Rational{UInt8}}:
 0x02//0x07
 0x06//0x07
 0x00//0x01
 0x00//0x01
 0x06//0x07
 0x00//0x01
 0x03//0x07
 0x03//0x07
 0x02//0x07
 0x00//0x01
```

The alternative, which is much nicer to use (largely due to hooking into `rand` interface), is:
```julia
abstract type RationalInterval{T<:Integer} end
struct CloseClose01_m{T<:Integer, S<:Integer} <: RationalInterval{T}
    m::S
    global unsafe_closeclose01_m(::Type{T}, m::S) where {T,S} = new{T,S}(m)
end
function CloseClose01_m{T}(m::S) where {T<:Unsigned, S<:Integer}
    0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof(T) || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof($T)"))
    unsafe_closeclose01_m(T, m)
end
function CloseClose01_m{T}(m::S) where {T<:Signed, S<:Integer}
    0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof(T)-1 || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof($T)-1"))
    unsafe_closeclose01_m(T, m)
end
Base.eltype(::Type{S}) where {S<:RationalInterval{T}} where {T<:Integer} = Rational{T}
function rand(r::AbstractRNG, sp::SamplerTrivial{CloseClose01_m{T, S}}) where {T<:Union{UInt8,UInt16,UInt32,UInt64}, S<:Integer}
    m = sp[].m
    x = rand(r, UInt64) >>> (64 - m) % T
    y = one(T) << m - one(T)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
function rand(r::AbstractRNG, sp::SamplerTrivial{CloseClose01_m{T, S}}) where {T<:Union{Int8,Int16,Int32,Int64}, S<:Integer}
    m = sp[].m
    x = rand(r, UInt64) >>> (64 - m) % T
    y = typemax(T) >> (8*sizeof(T)-1 - m)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
function rand(r::AbstractRNG, sp::SamplerTrivial{CloseClose01_m{UInt128, S}}) where {S<:Integer}
    m = sp[].m
    x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m)
    y = one(UInt128) << m - one(UInt128)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
function rand(r::AbstractRNG, sp::SamplerTrivial{CloseClose01_m{Int128, S}}) where {S<:Integer}
    m = sp[].m
    x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m) % Int128
    y = typemax(Int128) >> (127 - m)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
```
This yields an expressive means of sampling any combination of `n` and `m`.
```julia
julia> xo = Xoshiro(1234);

julia> cc_64_63 = CloseClose01_m{UInt64}(0x3f)
CloseClose01_m{UInt64, UInt8}(0x3f)

julia> rand(xo, cc_63_64)
0x00d9fdf04da6f0d8//0x029cbc14e5e0a72f

julia> ccu_8_3 = CloseClose01_m{UInt8}(0x03)
CloseClose01_m{UInt8, UInt8}(0x03)

julia> rand(ccu_8_3, 5, 2)
5×2 Matrix{Rational{UInt8}}:
 0x00//0x01  0x05//0x07
 0x04//0x07  0x01//0x07
 0x05//0x07  0x05//0x07
 0x02//0x07  0x02//0x07
 0x01//0x01  0x05//0x07

julia> cci_8_3 = CloseClose01_m{Int8}(3)
CloseClose01_m{Int8, Int64}(3)

julia> rand(cci_8_3, 5, 2)
5×2 Matrix{Rational{Int8}}:
 1//7  6//7
 1//7  4//7
 5//7  1//1
 1//7  6//7
 2//7  1//7
```



# On Morgenstern-2006
```julia
#### Morgenstern 2006 -- Section 1
# Some attempts with horrible seeds
julia> N = 10^6;

julia> g(t) = cos(2 * π * 10^10 * t);    # ∫₀¹ g(t)dt = 0

julia> g²(t) = abs2(g(t));               # ∫₀¹ g²(t)dt = 0.5

julia> mc(r, f, N) = sum(f(rand(r)) for _ = 1:N) / N;

julia> xo = Xoshiro(0x1);

julia> mc(xo, g, N)

-0.0002710728024089284

julia> mc(xo, g², N)
0.5002416981725267

julia> mt = MersenneTwister(0x1);

julia> mc(mt, g, N)
0.0007549637067530266

julia> mc(mt, g², N)
0.5002242685930319

julia> mutable struct LCG10{A<:Integer, S<:Integer, M<:Integer}
           a::A
           s::S
           m::M
       end;

julia> rand(r::LCG10, ::Type{Float64}) = _next(r) / r.m;

julia> rand(r::LCG10, ::Type{T}) where {T<:AbstractFloat} = T(_next(r)) / T(r.m);

julia> rand(r::LCG10) = rand(r, Float64);

julia> function _next(r::LCG10)
           (; a, s, m) = r
           s = a * s % m
           r.s = s
           s
       end;

julia> lcg10 = LCG10(UInt(427419669081), UInt(0x1), UInt(999999999989));

julia> mc(lcg10, g, N)

0.0003320382805466088

julia> mc(lcg10, g², N)
0.49994037168781214

julia> sum(g(Float32(rand(lcg10))) for _ = 1:N) / N
0.000762443525496333

julia> sum(g²(Float32(rand(lcg10))) for _ = 1:N) / N
0.4999173715876127

julia> sum(g(Float16(rand(lcg10))) for _ = 1:N) / N
-0.003556871162906289

julia> sum(g²(Float16(rand(lcg10))) for _ = 1:N) / N # Well, barbaric rounding gets us somewhere...
0.7494309231800658

julia> setprecision(BigFloat, 10, base=10)
10

julia> sum(g(BigFloat(rand(lcg10))) for _ = 1:N) / N # To be fair, not everything is same precision
0.00255783884813

julia> sum(g²(BigFloat(rand(lcg10))) for _ = 1:N) / N
0.498719404946

julia> g(t, ::Type{T}) where {T<:AbstractFloat} = cos(2 * T(π) * 10^10 * t);

julia> g²(t, ::Type{T}) where {T<:AbstractFloat} = abs2(g(t, T));

julia> sum(g(rand(lcg10, BigFloat), BigFloat) for _ = 1:N) / N  # well, puzzling
-0.00287454071531

julia> sum(g²(rand(lcg10, BigFloat), BigFloat) for _ = 1:N) / N
0.501611169253
```
The above is just a superficial reproduction of the math, but not necessarily the exact arithmetic Maple 10 would have used. Could this explain my inability to reproduce? Noting that I have never used Maple, but have used MATLAB extensively, I have a hunch that Maple 10's decimal floating point was doing some magic behind the scenes (to force certain mathematical identities, which are true of real numbers, but not floats). One would have a reasonable expectation that BigFloat's (which obeys IEEE rounding) with precision set to 10 decimal digits should be able to elicit similar behavior. I believe I am in [good company](https://people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~wkahan/Mindless.pdf) in being suspicious of (decimal) floating point which does not explicitly conform to IEEE rounding. Note that I am not trying to dismiss my inability to reproduce Morgenstern's results, but without a Maple 10 from 2006, it's probably hopeless. 

On the subject of results in Section 1, they are partly dependent on the nature of LCG's. If we attempt to work in rationals as far as possible, carrying the relatively few denominators offered by Rational{Int} until the point at which we must use a float (cos), we do not observe the problem which seems to emanate from the LCG. (Though, this assumes that I could replicate the floating point behavior, which I can't).
```julia
julia> const πᵣ = rationalize(Int, π, tol=2^-62)
6167950454//1963319607

julia> 𝑔(t) = cos(2 * πᵣ * (10^10 * t));

julia> 𝑔²(t) = abs2(𝑔(t));

julia> sum(𝑔(Rational{BigInt}(rand(Rational{Int}))) for _ = 1:N) / N

-0.0009990287992112217632625414290644167914619440478086315875375977433841484769463525

julia> sum(𝑔²(Rational{BigInt}(rand(Rational{Int}))) for _ = 1:N) / N
0.5003065979441251943945511738233627901854680635895315259578590913865011799718236

julia> sum(g(rand(Rational{Int})) for _ = 1:N) / N

-0.0004671059707741984

julia> sum(g²(rand(Rational{Int})) for _ = 1:N) / N
0.5002582875480501
```

# On #25993 and "life outside the unit interval"
Said thread contains some interesting questions. Whether a uniform distribution can be defined for rationals, rather than just for computer representations of rationals is a thorny question. It is clear that for representations involving n-bit integers with finite n, on the unit interval, a uniform distribution can always be defined. Let us suppose that we have draws from the unit interval, given some n; these are u ~ U(0,1). If we wished instead to have samples from the rationals on [c, d], applying the transformation v = c + (d-c)*u is equivalent to applying the transformation v1 = (d-c)*u, which we can recognize as U(a(d-c), b(d-c)), where (a=0,b=1) are the original lower/upper bounds, respectively, then applying the transformation v2 = c + v1, which has no effect on the probability mass function, but simply relocates such that U(c + a(d-c), c + b(d-c)). Therefore, the resultant distribution would be uniform, with a discretization which is identical to that achieved on the unit interval. Most likely, one would need to widen the storage to achieve the re-scaling without causing the original size integers to overflow. We note that widening the storage does not affect the discretization achieved by a generation procedure on n-bit rationals; this is identical to the m < n case above, in which one generates m-bit rationals but stores them directly in an n-bit integer. That is, if we generate an n-bit rational then widen later, we just need to remember the original size in order to determine what discretization was achieved.

-- this is perhaps not the final word on this last section; it was written at the end of a long day.

Thoughts, @mschauer, @simonbyrne ?

# Continuation of: On #25993 -- shifting and scaling
Admittedly, this is a bit mundane, but for completeness: equivalent discretization to that achieved on the unit interval, but for any interval. Note that I do not actually necessarily recommend this type of practice; or, at least, avoid mixing two or more discretizations within one program (but the same is true of mixing rand(::Float32) and rand(::Float64))

##
If r is an n-bit rational on the unit interval, the discretization of said interval is into 2^n pieces, with lower bound 0/(2^n - 1) and upper bound (2^n - 1)/(2^n - 1). The original distance between points is 1/(2^n - 1), such that the sequence is: 
0, 1/(2^n - 1), 2/(2^n - 1), …, (2^n - 2)/(2^n - 1), (2^n - 1)/(2^n - 1).

If one relocates by adding a constant, c, the lower bound becomes c*(2^n - 1)/(2^n - 1) and the upper bound (c+1)*(2^n - 1)/(2^n - 1). The discretization is unaffected; it still contains 2^n pieces. The distance between points remains 1/(2^n - 1), but the sequence becomes: 
c, c + 1/(2^n - 1), c + 2/(2^n - 1), …, c + (2^n - 2)/(2^n - 1), c + (2^n - 1)/(2^n - 1).

If one re-scales by multiplying by a constant, d, the lower bound remains 0/(2^n - 1), but the upper bound becomes d*(2^n - 1)/(2^n - 1). The distance between points becomes d/(2^n - 1), such that the sequence becomes: 
0, d/(2^n - 1), 2d/(2^n - 1), …, d*(2^n - 2)/(2^n - 1), d*(2^n - 1)/(2^n - 1). 

If one wanted to achieve the same discretization as the unit interval, one would need to use a denominator d*(2^n - 1), such that the distance between points becomes d/(d*(2^n - 1)) = 1/(2^n - 1). If d*(2^n - 1) can be expressed as an equivalent n′-bit integer, this greatly facilitates matters. d = 2^n + 1 is a convenient example, as (2^n + 1) * (2^n - 1) = 2^2n - 1, thus, if n=32, then we can achieve an equivalent discretization on the new interval by using n′=64. Clearly, it is exceedingly rare for things to work out so nicely, or even to yield a denominator which can be expressed as 2^n′ - 1. It is the generation of the numerator which causes difficulty; one might consider (and has little other recourse than) an accept-reject algorithm to handle denominators which cannot be expressed as 2^n′ - 1. If we say n=3, d=6, then 1/7 is the original distance, for which the scaled distance would be 6/7. To achieve the same discretization, one needs a new denominator, d*(2^n - 1) = 42 = 0b00101010 = 0x2a. There are 42 feasible bit patterns ≤ 0x2a, and assuming a uniform stream of bits, the distribution on them is uniform. We can improve the efficiency of the accept-reject step by discarding all integers which are > 0b00111111; that is, we need only consider random integers which have ≤ leading zeros than d*(2^n - 1). Or, in other words, random m′-bit integers where m′ = n + ceil(Int, log2(d)).

Some toys for convenient exploration -- just like the post above, I do not necessarily intend to incorporate these into base unless requested to do so.
```julia
using Random
import Random: rand, default_rng, SamplerTrivial
import Base: checked_mul, divgcd, unsafe_rational

abstract type RationalInterval{T<:Integer} end
Base.eltype(::Type{S}) where {S<:RationalInterval{T}} where {T<:Integer} = Rational{T}

denom(::Type{T}, m::Integer, d::Integer) where {T<:Signed} = checked_mul(T(d), typemax(T) >> (8*sizeof(T)-1 - m)) % T
denom(::Type{T}, m::Integer, d::Integer) where {T<:Unsigned} = checked_mul(T(d), (one(T) << m - one(T))) % T
struct CloseClose0d{T<:Integer, S<:Integer} <: RationalInterval{T}
    m::S # size of original m-bit integer
    d::S # rescaling factor for interval, e.g. [0,1] → [0,6] ⟹ d=6
    y::T # d*(2^8sizeof(T) - 1)
    m′::S # number of active bit positions, i.e. size of rescaled -bit integer
    global unsafe_closeclose0d(::Type{T}, m::S, d::S, y::T, m′::S) where {T,S} = new{T,S}(m,d,y,m′)
end
function CloseClose0d{T}(m::S, d::S) where {T<:Unsigned, S<:Integer}
    n = 8*sizeof(T)
    0 < m ≤ n || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof($T)"))
    y = denom(T, m, d)
    m′ = (n - leading_zeros(y)) % S
    unsafe_closeclose0d(T, m, d, y, m′)
end
function CloseClose0d{T}(m::S, d::S) where {T<:Signed, S<:Integer}
    n = 8*sizeof(T)
    0 < m ≤ n-1 || throw(ArgumentError("m must be 0 < m ≤ 8*sizeof($T)-1"))
    y = denom(T, m, d)
    m′ = (n - leading_zeros(y)) % S
    unsafe_closeclose0d(T, m, d, y, m′)
end

function acceptreject(r::AbstractRNG, ::Type{T}, y::T, m′::Integer) where {T<:Integer}
    x = rand(r, UInt64) >>> (64 - m′) % T
    while x > y
        x = rand(r, UInt64) >>> (64 - m′) % T
    end
    x
end
function acceptreject(r::AbstractRNG, ::Type{UInt128}, y::T, m′::Integer)
    x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m′)
    while x > y
        x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m′)
    end
    x
end
function acceptreject(r::AbstractRNG, ::Type{Int128}, y::T, m′::Integer)
    x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m′) % Int128
    while x > y
        x = rand(r, UInt128) >>> (128 - m′) % Int128
    end
    x
end
acceptreject(::Type{T}, y::T, m′::Integer) where {T<:Integer} = acceptreject(default_rng(), T, y, m′)

function rand(r::AbstractRNG, sp::SamplerTrivial{CloseClose0d{T, S}}) where {T<:Integer, S<:Integer}
    (; m, d, y, m′)= sp[]
    x = acceptreject(r, T, y, m′)
    num, den = divgcd(x, y)
    unsafe_rational(num, den)
end
```
With which we can do things like
```julia
julia> cci_8_3_6 = CloseClose0d{Int8}(3, 6)
CloseClose0d{Int8, Int64}(3, 6, 42, 6)

julia> rand(cci_8_3_6, 3,3)
3×3 Matrix{Rational{Int8}}:
 1//21  6//7   1//1
 1//6   2//21  2//21
 1//21  5//21  0//1

julia> ccu_8_5_7 = CloseClose0d{UInt8}(5, 7)
CloseClose0d{UInt8, Int64}(5, 7, 0xd9, 8)

julia> rand(ccu_8_5_7, 3,3)
3×3 Matrix{Rational{UInt8}}:
 0x92//0xd9  0xd1//0xd9  0x01//0x1f
 0x9c//0xd9  0xb5//0xd9  0xb2//0xd9
 0x5f//0xd9  0xc8//0xd9  0x11//0xd9

`BitVector` from unsigned integers, and l/r-`shift[!]` methods which
match the corresponding shifts on bit indices.

When writing the code for `BitVector(x::Unsigned)`, I realized
that the shift operators (`>>`, `>>>`, `<<`) have the opposite
behavior w.r.t. the corresponding bitshifts. Odd, I thought, then
found [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30958) --
sufficient motivation to write the l/r-`shift[!]`
methods. Incidentally, these shifts happen to be quite useful when
testing the behavior of the construction-from-unsigned, which is why
these two concepts are initially being submitted as part of the same
PR.

Admittedly, I would normally have preferred to submit separate ideas
as separate PRs so as to facilitate rejecting one or the other; my
apologies for the inconvenience.

Miscellaneous: the implementation of the l/r-`shift[!]` methods
enables copy-free, in-place shifts on `BitVector`s, but it is not as
efficient as it possibly could be -- there are some partial passes
over the backing `Vector{UInt64}` which could probably be eliminated
by properly re-writing `copy_chunks_rshift!`. I also notice the
existence of a potentially dangerous but gainful opportunity for
`@inbounds` in In fact, `op` never gets called; for example:
```julia
julia> mybinaryop(a, b) = (println("hello world!"); a + b)
mybinaryop (generic function with 1 method)

julia> reduce(mybinaryop, [1])
1

julia> reduce(mybinaryop, [1,2])
hello world!
3

julia> reduce(mybinaryop, [1,2,3])
hello world!
hello world!
6
```

To be fair, the docstring for `reduce` with kwarg `dims` states that the `init` kwarg is optional only for `+`, `*`, `max` and `min`.

Constructing initial values can be quite difficult -- the neutral element cannot necessarily be derived from the array and binary operator alone, hence the error thrown.

`BitVector` from unsigned integers, and l/r-`shift[!]` methods which
match the corresponding shifts on bit indices.

When writing the code for `BitVector(x::Unsigned)`, I realized
that the shift operators (`>>`, `>>>`, `<<`) have the opposite
behavior w.r.t. the corresponding bitshifts. Odd, I thought, then
found [this](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30958) --
sufficient motivation to write the l/r-`shift[!]`
methods. Incidentally, these shifts happen to be quite useful when
testing the behavior of the construction-from-unsigned, which is why
these two concepts are initially being submitted as part of the same
PR.

Admittedly, I would normally have preferred to submit separate ideas
as separate PRs so as to facilitate rejecting one or the other; my
apologies for the inconvenience.

Miscellaneous: the implementation of the l/r-`shift[!]` methods
enables copy-free, in-place shifts on `BitVector`s, but it is not as
efficient as it possibly could be -- there are some partial passes
over the backing `Vector{UInt64}` which could probably be eliminated
by properly re-writing `copy_chunks_rshift!`. I also notice the
existence of a potentially dangerous but gainful opportunity for
`@inbounds` in `_msk_rtol!`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45728/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45728/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1159593285","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45728#issuecomment-1159593285","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45728","id":1159593285,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5FHf1F","user":{"login":"andrewjradcliffe","id":96091198,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbo8Pg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96091198?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe","html_url":"https://github.com/andrewjradcliffe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-19T01:16:52Z","updated_at":"2022-06-19T01:16:52Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545","id":1256492451,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5K5I2j","number":45545,"title":"Long compilation time for simple Tuple function","user":{"login":"mtfishman","id":7855256,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc4NTUyNTY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7855256?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman","html_url":"https://github.com/mtfishman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mtfishman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-06-01T16:53:37Z","updated_at":"2022-06-20T20:11:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I wrote a simple function for splitting a Tuple into a Tuple of Tuples according to a function that specifies where the Tuple should be split, and it has surprisingly long compilation time:
```julia
julia> function tuple_split(f, t::Tuple)
         n = findall(f, t)
         ti = t[1:(first(n) - 1)]
         ts = ntuple(i -> t[n[i]:(n[i + 1] - 1)], length(n) - 1)
         tf = t[last(n):end]
         return (ti, ts..., tf)
       end
tuple_split (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @time tuple_split(x -> x isa String, (1, "X", 1, "Y", 2))
  9.194670 seconds (2.36 M allocations: 98.959 MiB, 0.34% gc time, 100.00% compilation time)
((1,), ("X", 1), ("Y", 2))

julia> @time tuple_split(x -> x isa String, (1, "X", 1, "Y", 2))
  0.037311 seconds (88.58 k allocations: 4.821 MiB, 99.80% compilation time)
((1,), ("X", 1), ("Y", 2))

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.7.3
Commit 742b9abb4d (2022-05-06 12:58 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
  CPU: Intel(R) Xeon(R) E-2176M  CPU @ 2.70GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-12.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
Environment:
  JULIA_EDITOR = vim
```
The same issue appears in Julia 1.8:
```julia
julia> @time tuple_split(x -> x isa String, (1, "X", 1, "Y", 2))
  8.476479 seconds (2.34 M allocations: 89.287 MiB, 1.60% gc time, 99.98% compilation time)
((1,), ("X", 1), ("Y", 2))

julia> @time tuple_split(x -> x isa String, (1, "X", 1, "Y", 2))
  0.038055 seconds (86.86 k allocations: 4.532 MiB, 99.77% compilation time)
((1,), ("X", 1), ("Y", 2))

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.8.0-rc1
Commit 6368fdc656 (2022-05-27 18:33 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-pc-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 6 × Intel(R) Xeon(R) E-2176M  CPU @ 2.70GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-13.0.1 (ORCJIT, skylake)
  Threads: 1 on 6 virtual cores
Environment:
  LD_PRELOAD = libgtk3-nocsd.so.0
  LD_LIBRARY_PATH = /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcutensor/11.1/:/opt/intel/compilers_and_libraries_2020.0.166/linux/tbb/lib/intel64_lin/gcc4.7:/opt/intel/compilers_and_libraries_2020.0.166/linux/compiler/lib/intel64_lin:/opt/intel/compilers_and_libraries_2020.0.166/linux/mkl/lib/intel64_lin:/usr/local/cuda-11.6/lib64::/home/mfishman/software/itensor/lib
  JULIA_EDITOR = vim
```

For my use case I worked around this by splitting into a `Vector{Tuple}` instead of a Tuple of Tuples since the performance wasn't so important, but I thought I would report this here since the compilation time was surprisingly long.

Also note that the implementation shown above isn't type stable so probably isn't the best way to write it to begin with.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1160811435","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545#issuecomment-1160811435","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45545","id":1160811435,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5FMJOr","user":{"login":"andrewjradcliffe","id":96091198,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbo8Pg","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/96091198?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe","html_url":"https://github.com/andrewjradcliffe","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/andrewjradcliffe/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-06-20T20:11:40Z","updated_at":"2022-06-20T20:11:40Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"An alternative to `Vector{Tuple}`:

```julia
julia> function tuple_split(n::NTuple{N,Int}, t::Tuple) where {N}
                ti = t[1:(first(n) - 1)]
                ts = ntuple(i -> t[n[i]:(n[i + 1] - 1)], Val(N-1))
                tf = Base.rest(t, last(n))
                return (ti, ts..., tf)
       end
tuple_split (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> tuple_split(f, t::Tuple) = tuple_split(Tuple(findall(f, t)), t)

`@eval $fun(x::AbstractSparseVector{T}) where {T} = $fun(identity, x)` to (at time of writing) line 1440 in SparseArrays/src/sparsevector.jl -- my thought is that is is a fair dispatch to add, given the analogous dispatches for AbstactSparseMatrixCSC. Testing on a local build demonstrated that this resolved the issue, but I do not know quite how to proceed (this has become a complicated first PR ^_^;;). I have submitted a PR for the proposed change in SparseArrays (https://github.com/JuliaSparse/SparseArrays.jl/pull/94). Assuming that PR goes through, I think this PR will be non-failing from the Test perspective.

However, the errors in SparseArrays made me aware of the possible need to cover `findmin(f, A::AbstractSparseMatrixCSC; dims)`, and `findmin(A::AbstractSparseMatrixCSC; dims)`. Note that the current the dispatch in SparseArrays is not mangled by the above proposal. To my knowledge, the `findmin(A::AbstractSparseMatrixCSC, region::Union{Integer,Tuple{Integer},NTuple{2,Integer}})` is not exactly publicly documented, so perhaps this is not so much of an issue for JuliaLang:master as is is for SparseArrays. 

I would appreciate input on this -- inspection of the code in SparseArrays reveals that it would not be too difficult to handle. In fact, this PR's code works on the SparseArrays (but it's far less efficient, and the returned type is a SparseMatrix, which is part of the problem).
Using the code from this PR:
```
julia> y = sprand(Float64, 100,100, 0.3);

julia> findmax(y, (1,)) == findmax(identity, y, dims=(1,))
true

julia> findmax(y, (1,2)) == findmax(identity, y, dims=(1,2))
true

julia> findmax(y, (2,)) == findmax(identity, y, dims=(2,))
true

julia> y = sprand(Float64, 100,100, 0.001);

julia> findmax(y, 1) == findmax(identity, y, dims=1)
true

julia> findmax(y, (1,2)) == findmax(identity, y, dims=(1,2))
true

julia> findmax(y, (2,)) == findmax(identity, y, dims=(2,))
true

julia> @benchmark findmax(y, 2)
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 9 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  2.758 μs …  1.034 ms  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 99.48%
 Time  (median):     2.896 μs              ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   3.360 μs ± 12.025 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  6.52% ±  2.20%

 Memory estimate: 3.41 KiB, allocs estimate: 41.

julia> @benchmark findmax(identity, y, dims=2)
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1 evaluation.
 Range (min … max):  69.196 μs … 305.165 μs  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     74.247 μs               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   74.130 μs ±   3.145 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

 Memory estimate: 2.36 KiB, allocs estimate: 13.

julia> typeof(findmax(y, 2))
Tuple{Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{CartesianIndex{2}}}

julia> typeof(findmax(identity, y, dims=2))
Tuple{SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int64}, Matrix{CartesianIndex{2}}}
```

Thus, I believe that there's a need to handle this a bit more elegantly than just adding a dispatch such as `_findmin(f, A::AbstractSparseMatrixCSC, dims) = findmin(A, dims)`, which ignores the function argument completely. From a quick glance, it should be possible to enable a function argument in the same manner as for non-sparse arrays. I will work on this while awaiting feedback.

A detailed description of thoughts on changes to the `findmin`/`findmax` interface and dispatches can be found at: https://github.com/JuliaSparse/SparseArrays.jl/pull/94#issuecomment-1107931026

In hindsight, this was probably better posted as a comment on this issue, thus, I reproduce it here for posterity, with edits for relevance.

Current Base interface + dispatches for findmin/findmax:

```
findmax(A::AbstractArray; dims=:) = _findmax(A, dims)
findmax(f, domain) = mapfoldl( ((k, v),) -> (f(v), k), _rf_findmax, pairs(domain) )
findmax(itr) = _findmax(itr, :)
_findmax(a, ::Colon) = findmax(identity, a)
_findmax(A, region) # region being Union{Integer, NTuple{M, Integer}} where {M}

```
In the above scheme, the call to `findmax(A)` (with `A::AbstractSparseVector`) would have first been through `findmax(A::AbstractArray; dims=:)`, which would redirect to `_findmin(a, ::Colon)`, thereby calling `findmax(identity, a)`, which then dispatches to `findmax(f, A::AbstractSparseVector{T}) where {T}`
While this works, it precludes the use of a function argument for multidimensional findmin/findmax. Hence, in the original commit, I was proposing the interface + dispatches described below:

Option 1 (not used) of new Base interface + dispatches for findmin/findmax:

```
findmax(A::AbstractArray; dims=:) = _findmax(identity, A, dims)
findmax(f, A::AbstractArray; dims=:) = _findmax(f, A, dims)
findmax(f, domain) = _findmax(f, domain, :)
findmax(itr) = findmax(identity, itr)
_findmax(f, domain, ::Colon) = mapfoldl( ((k, v),) -> (f(v), k), _rf_findmax, pairs(domain) )
_findmax(f, A, region) # region being Union{Integer, NTuple{M, Integer}} where {M}

```
Under those changes, the call to `findmax(A)` (with `A::AbstractSparseVector`) never has the chance to dispatch on `findmax(f, A::AbstractSparseVector)`. I must admit, the whole situation is awkward. If we consider a hypothetical new user, adding a dispatch which provides an optimized method for `findmax` for some subtype of AbstractArray, they would probably think that adding `findmax(f, A::SomeSpecialArray)` would be sufficient. My change would force them to also add `findmax(A::SomeSpecialArray) = findmax(identity, A)`.

Instead, it seems preferable to keep the original interface + dispatch intact, while incorporating the new ideas.

Option 2 (current proposal) of new Base interface + dispatches for findmin/findmax:

```
findmax(A::AbstractArray; dims=:) = _findmax(A, dims)
findmax(f, A::AbstractArray; dims=:) = _findmax(f, A, dims)
findmax(f, domain) = _findmax(f, domain, :)
findmax(itr) = _findmax(itr, :) # or, equivalently, findmax(identity, itr)
_findmax(a, ::Colon) = findmax(identity, a)
_findmax(A, dims) = _findmax(identity, A, dims)
_findmax(f, A, ::Colon) = mapfoldl( ((k, v),) -> (f(v), k), _rf_findmax, pairs(domain) )
_findmax(f, A, region) # region being Union{Integer, NTuple{M, Integer}} where {M}
```

This approach allows our hypothetical user to get the behavior they expect by defining just findmax(f, A::SomeSpecialArray).

Thoughts:

- Option 1 will be more disturbing to users which are already relying on the extant behavior to define new dispatches.
- Deducing that Option 1 necessitates `findmax(A::SomeSpecialArray) = findmax(identity, A)` is probably non-trivial for a newer user. For experienced users, it begs the question: why is this not handled through a generic?

Having worked through this with a clear head, I don't see much reason to consider anything other than Option 2. The PR has been amended to reflect this.
